retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Etiology -- Genetic Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Nonsyndromic monogenic causes: SHH anomalies are the most common defects recorded in HPE patients with associated midline defects. Other leading genetic causes include ZIC2, SIX3, and TGIF. [7] The main functions of these genes include the following: Zinc finger protein ( ZIC2 ) - Arrangement of the anterior notochord SIX homeobox 3 ( SIX3 ) - Arrangement of the anterior-posterior polarity of brain vesicles Sonic hedgehog ( SHH ) - Telencephalic vesicle and optic cup patterning from the neural tube's ventral midline section Bone morphogenetic protein - Craniofacial patterning Fibroblast growth factor - Craniofacial patterning TG interacting factors (Tgifs) 1 and 2 - Regulation of SHH signaling [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Extensively researched cases of HPE primarily focus on individuals with normal karyotypes but with chromosomal anomalies, and these cases seldom coincide with malformation syndromes. In most live births affected by HPE, the condition is classified as nonsyndromic and nonchromosomal, indicating that it cannot be ascribed to syndromic or chromosomal origins. This category encompasses environmental causes, deletions, or gene mutations that play a role in HPE or unidentified factors. The etiology of HPE can be divided into several categories, as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of HPE is genetic or sporadic. Genetic causes can be further classified into syndromic and nonsyndromic causes.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Rectum and Anal Canal -- Molecular Level -- Homeobox Gene
retrieved_docs.content: The HOX genes are expressed along the lateral plate mesoderm of the GI tract during development and might play a role in the growth and differentiation of the epithelium. Some mice studies have shown that misaligning sequences of these genes or ectopic expression of hox genes have resulted in the development of hamartomas in the gastric mucosa. Inactivation of some hox genes has correlated with absence anal sphincters, lumen blockage, and disruption of GI musculature, which demonstrates the importance of these genes in the development of the GI tract. [6] [7] The cranio-caudal development of the gut and differentiation into various organs depends on the expression of many genes, including the hox genes. The expression of these genes appears to be highly regulated by the concentration gradient of retinoic acid. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Vertebral Column Development -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: VACTERL stands for vertebral anomalies (V), anal atresia (A), cardiac malformation (C), tracheoesophageal fistula (TE) with or without esophageal atresia, renal dysplasia (R) and limb abnormalities (L). VACTERL is a purely clinical diagnosis that is made by patients having three or more of the listed anomalies. The most common defect found in VACTERL patients is vertebral anomalies – found to be reported in 60-95% of VACTERL patients. Vertebral anomalies seen include failure of formation and/or segmentation of vertebrae. The exact genetic causes of VACTERL syndrome are not fully understood, but some studies have shown a possible association with aberrant expression of Hox / SHH genes. [4] Spina bifida

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Pertinent clinical points relevant to HPE are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the relationship between the development of the face and the brain to comprehend the pathogenesis of HPE is essential. The successful development of the brain and face occurs due to the reciprocal signaling of several molecules between the surface ectoderm and neural crest cells that determine the migration of primitive embryonal cells, leading to the formation of highly developed structures. Incomplete diverticulation and cleavage of the embryonic forebrain is the reason for the intracranial pathology. Blocking this signaling alters the dorsoventral polarity and anterior-posterior axis and inhibits bilateral symmetry of the forebrain. SHH is 1 such signaling molecule. Inhibiting SHH expression in the basal telencephalon subsequently impedes the dorsoventral polarity and inhibits the induction of SHH expression in the frontonasal ectodermal zone (FEZ), leading to multiple midline facial defects. Various degrees of a block in the Shh signaling determine the severity of the disease manifestation.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Vertebral Column Development -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Of utmost importance to correct vertebral column development is proper gene expression and cell signaling that allows each vertebra to take the appropriate shape in the anterior-posterior and cranio-caudal direction. One of these genes, known as Hox, is responsible for the proper orientation of the vertebrae in the cranio-caudal direction. [3] The SHH gene is another gene found to play an essential role in anterior-posterior development and ventral neural tube and somite patterning. [4] Many genes have been and continue to be discovered that will further our understanding of this process and potentially how errors in specific genes and their associated signaling pathways could result in common congenital anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Etiology -- Genetic Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal Dominant: CDON gene-related Steinfield syndrome FGFR1 gene-related Kallman syndrome and Hartsfield syndrome (holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and palate, and ectrodactyly) Agnathia-Otocephaly Complex (gene PRRX1) Culler-Jones syndrome CHARGE syndrome Autosomal Recessive: CENPF gene-related Stromme syndrome DHCR7 gene-related Smith-Lemli-Opitz Syndrome - intellectual disability, dysmorphisms of the face, congenital cardiac anomalies, and male external genitalia defects [12] [13] HHAT (encoding Hedgehog acyltransferase) PLCH1 (encoding phospholipase C eta-1) STIL [encoding a factor required for maintenance of primary cilia) [3] Hydrolethalus syndrome ( HYLS1 and KIF7 genes ) Genoa syndrome Lambotte syndrome X-linked: X-linked holoprosencephaly-13 ( HPE13 ) ( STAG2 ) [14] Variants in genes: Driver genes: Essential to the phenotype of the patient carrying a variant- SHH , ZIC2 , SIX3 , FGF8 , and FGFR1 Silent modifier gene: BOC Predisposing genes: All other genes responsible

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing is recommended for parents with children showing apparent symptoms or for those showing mild variants. [12] However, the indication for testing depends on the disease's clinical suspicion, which is very difficult given the numerous phenotypic variations. The number of genes involved and a multi-hit process compound the challenging diagnosis further. The de-novo occurrence of some mutations that show a specific defect in the parents has also been observed. In these cases, the chances of HPE in a subsequent pregnancy are significantly reduced. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Central Nervous System, Malformations -- Clinical Significance -- Holoprosencephaly
retrieved_docs.content: Holoprosencephaly is a malformation of the prosencephalon characterized by incomplete separation of both cerebral hemispheres. Chromosomal abnormalities such as Patau and Edward syndromes carry a higher risk for holoprosencephaly as well as gestation complicated with diabetes. Patau syndrome (trisomy 13) is the most commonly associated syndrome. [1] [12] Holoprosencephaly is usually incompatible with life, and most children born with this malformation have very high mortality early in postnatal life. The subtypes of holoprosencephaly in order of increasing severity are middle interhemispheric, lobar, semi-lobar, and alobar variants. A brain CT scan or MRI can confirm the diagnosis and differentiate the subtypes of holoprosencephaly. [13] Genes like the Bone Morphogenetic Protein (BMP), Sonic hedgehog (Shh), Fibroblast Growth Factor (FGF) are all suspected to be associated with holoprosencephaly. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing is carried out to determine the existence of any chromosomal anomaly by conducting cytogenetic and molecular testing. If this reveals any chromosomal or genetic anomaly associated with HPE, it further mandates genetic counseling for the parents should they decide to conceive another child. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing is recommended for parents with a child showing apparent symptoms or for those showing mild variants.

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Incomplete penetrance and variable expressivity make predicting disease manifestation in offspring difficult. Parents carrying defined mutations may not have either physical or neurological symptoms. Current studies suggest a multi-hit origin of HPE, explaining the lack of clinical manifestations in patients with the concerned mutations. [17] [18] [19] [20] Basal forebrain patterning occurs during the third and fourth week of embryogenesis. This directs the cleavage of the prosencephalon during the fifth and sixth weeks of gestation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Holoprosencephaly (HPE) occurs due to failed cerebral hemisphere separation and is associated with forebrain and facial midline defects. [12] The condition may result from SHH signaling dysfunction or altered bone morphogenic proteins (BMPs).

retrieved_docs.title: Supernumerary Digit -- Evaluation -- Pallister-Hall Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Polydactyly, a bifid epiglottis, decreased pituitary function, imperforate anus, and a hypothalamic hamartoma are all characteristic features of Pallister-Hall Syndrome (PHS). PHS is an autosomal dominant disorder attributed to abnormalities in the GLI3 gene.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) is described as having a broader relationship with an unspecified condition that originated in the perinatal period (حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة), indicating that fetal growth restriction can be viewed as a more general clinical category under which certain undefined perinatal conditions may be classified, underscoring the importance of thorough assessment of growth abnormalities around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314

retrieved_docs.content: temporal occurrence during the fetal period, providing a detailed framework for understanding how diverse maternal and fetal conditions can impact labor and delivery outcomes and guiding clinicians in assessing and managing these complex scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: that relate to fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction and malposition; malpresentation, respectively. Collectively, these entities and relationships emphasize the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment, careful monitoring of fetal presentation and pelvic dimensions, avoidance of contraindicated medications, and individualized delivery planning to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of
